title: Fundamentals of Robotics
text:
Fundamentals of Robotics
by Rakshan V P

Preface
Welcome to a comprehensive journey into the mechanics and intelligence of machines. This book is designed to bridge the gap between theoretical physics and practical engineering, providing a rigorous foundation for the next generation of roboticists.


🦾 Kinematics
Explore the geometry of motion. Learn how to describe the position and orientation of multi-link systems without the complexity of forces.


⚙️ Dynamics
Understand the “why” behind the move. We dive into Newton-Euler and Lagrangian mechanics to model the forces that drive robotic motion.


🧠 Control
The brain of the system. Master the algorithms—from PID to State-Space—that allow machines to interact with the world with precision.



How to Use This Book

Sequential Learning: Chapters are designed to be read in order, as each builds upon the mathematical foundations of the previous.
Interactive Examples: Look for the “Code” blocks throughout the text to see how these concepts are implemented in Python and MATLAB.
Visual Intuition: Prioritize understanding the diagrams before diving into the derivations.




“Robotics is the intersection of mechanics, electronics, and intelligence.”

title: 5  Forward Kinematics
text:
5.1 Problem Statement
Forward kinematics determines the position and orientation of the end-effector from given joint parameters.
Given: - Joint angles (revolute joints)
- Link lengths
Find: - End-effector position

title: 5  Forward Kinematics
text:
5.2 Planar 2-Link Manipulator
Consider a 2-link robot arm:

Link lengths: \(l_1, l_2\)

Joint angles: \(\theta_1, \theta_2\)

The end-effector coordinates are:
\[
x = l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\]
\[
y = l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\]

title: 5  Forward Kinematics
text:
5.3 Python Example


Code
import numpy as np

l1, l2 = 1.0, 1.0
theta1, theta2 = np.pi/4, np.pi/6

x = l1*np.cos(theta1) + l2*np.cos(theta1 + theta2)
y = l1*np.sin(theta1) + l2*np.sin(theta1 + theta2)

x, y


(np.float64(0.9659258262890685), np.float64(1.6730326074756157))

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.1.1 What Defines a Robot?
The definition of a robot has shifted from early mythological “automata” to sophisticated autonomous agents. To maintain academic and industrial consistency, we rely on the international standard:







“An actuated mechanism programmable in two or more axes with a degree of autonomy, moving within its environment, to perform intended tasks.”
ISO 8373:2021




This definition highlights that a robot is not merely a machine, but a system capable of Sensing its surroundings, Processing that information, and Acting physically upon the world.


2.1.2 Historical Milestones
The history of robotics is a timeline of increasing environmental complexity—moving from the predictable factory floor to the unpredictable terrain of Mars and, eventually, the human household. Figure 2.1 provides an interactive look at these turning points.


Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import pandas as pd

# --- TIMELINE DATA ---
data = [
    (1950, "Unimate", "The first industrial robot, automating heavy lifting at GM.", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unimate"),
    (1966, "Shakey", "The first mobile robot to perceive and reason about its surroundings.", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shakey_the_robot"),
    (1997, "Sojourner", "First wheeled vehicle to roam another planet (Mars).", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sojourner_(rover)"),
    (2011, "Curiosity", "Mars rover exploring Gale Crater with a complex scientific payload.", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curiosity_(rover)"),
    (2021, "Optimus", "Tesla's general-purpose bipedal humanoid robot project.", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Optimus_(robot)"),
    (2024, "Figure 01", "AI-first humanoid utilizing vision-language-action models.", "https://robotsguide.com/robots/figure")
]

df = pd.DataFrame(data, columns=["Year", "Event", "Description", "Link"])
df['y'] = [1, -1] * (len(df)//2) + ([1] if len(df)%2 else [])

# --- COLORS (Obsidian/Midnight Theme) ---
BG_MAIN      = "#0f172a"  
BG_CARD      = "#1e293b"  
ACCENT_CYAN  = "#38bdf8"  
TEXT_DIM     = "#94a3b8"  
BORDER_COLOR = "#334155"  

fig = go.Figure()

# 1. Timeline Base Line
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=[df['Year'].min()-5, df['Year'].max()+5], y=[0, 0],
    mode='lines', line=dict(color=BORDER_COLOR, width=2),
    hoverinfo='none'
))

for i, row in df.iterrows():
    # 2. Vertical Stems
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[row['Year'], row['Year']], y=[0, row['y']],
        mode='lines', line=dict(color=BORDER_COLOR, width=1),
        hoverinfo='none'
    ))
    
    # 3. Nodes (Circles)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[row['Year']], y=[0],
        mode='markers',
        marker=dict(size=12, color=ACCENT_CYAN, line=dict(width=4, color=BG_MAIN)),
        hovertext=f"<b>{row['Event']}</b><br>{row['Description']}",
        hoverinfo="text" 
    ))

    # 4. Styled Rounded Boxes (Simulated via high borderpad)
    label_text = (
        f"<span style='color: {TEXT_DIM}; font-size: 11px;'>{row['Year']}</span><br>"
        f"<b><a href='{row['Link']}' target='_blank' "
        f"style='color: white; text-decoration: none;'>{row['Event']}</a></b>"
    )
    
    fig.add_annotation(
        x=row['Year'], y=row['y'] * 1.6, # Slightly more offset for rounded look
        text=label_text,
        showarrow=False,
        font=dict(size=13),
        align="center",
        bgcolor=BG_CARD, 
        bordercolor=ACCENT_CYAN, # Using cyan for a subtle "glow" border
        borderwidth=1.5,
        borderpad=12,            # HIGH padding creates the rounded/capsule effect
        opacity=1
    )

# --- LAYOUT ---
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(showgrid=False, zeroline=False, showticklabels=False, fixedrange=True),
    yaxis=dict(showgrid=False, zeroline=False, showticklabels=False, range=[-4, 4], fixedrange=True),
    paper_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    plot_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    hoverlabel=dict(
        bgcolor=BG_CARD,
        font_size=13,
        font_color="white",
        bordercolor=ACCENT_CYAN,
        # Rounds the tooltip box as well
        namelength=-1
    ),
    margin=dict(l=10, r=10, t=10, b=10),
    showlegend=False,
    width=740,
    height=450
)

fig.show()




                            
                                            


Figure 2.1: Evolution of Robotics: From Unimate to Humanoids.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.2 Robot Components

2.2.1 Base
The base serves as the primary support structure of the robot, ensuring stability and load-bearing. It may be stationary (industrial manipulators) or mobile (wheeled/legged platforms).


Examples: Cast-iron bolted pedestals for factory robots or tracked chassis for military EOD robots.




2.2.2 Arms and Legs
These are the kinematic structures responsible for motion and reach. Robotic arms enable precise manipulation, while legs facilitate locomotion in uneven terrains.


Examples: A 6-DOF articulated arm for assembly lines or four-linked quadrupedal legs for search-and-rescue tasks.




2.2.3 Hand or End Effector
The end effector is the interface between the robot and its environment, designed for task-specific interaction such as gripping, welding, painting, or material handling.


Examples: Two-finger vacuum grippers for logistics, MIG welding torches, or specialized surgical tools.




2.2.4 Actuators
Actuators are responsible for motion generation, converting electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy into mechanical movement and driving the robot’s joints.


Examples: Brushless DC motors (electric), high-pressure fluid cylinders (hydraulic), or air-powered suction bellows (pneumatic).




2.2.5 Sensors
Sensors provide state and environmental feedback, enabling perception and closed-loop control by measuring position, velocity, force, and orientation.


Examples: LiDAR for spatial mapping, rotary encoders for joint angle tracking, and 6-axis force-torque sensors.




2.2.6 Controller
The controller acts as the decision-making unit, executing control algorithms and generating actuator commands based on sensor feedback.


Examples: Industrial PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) units or dedicated PID motor controllers.




2.2.7 Processor
The processor is the computational core that performs real-time calculations, estimation, and higher-level planning tasks.


Examples: NVIDIA Jetson modules for AI processing, Raspberry Pi for light logic, or high-performance Intel/AMD CPUs.




2.2.8 Software
Software defines the robot’s intelligence and behavior, including control logic, motion planning, perception algorithms, and communication.


Examples: ROS (Robot Operating System) frameworks, Python-based computer vision scripts, and C++ motion planning libraries.




2.2.9 Links
Links are rigid structural elements that connect joints and form the robot’s physical architecture, determining its geometry and workspace.


Examples: Carbon fiber tubes for lightweight drone frames or machined aluminum beams in industrial gantries.





2.2.10 Joints

Revolute Joint (R)
A revolute joint provides one degree of freedom, allowing rotational motion about a fixed axis similar to a hinge.


Code
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# --- CONFIGURATION ---
CUBOID_W, CUBOID_H, LINK_L = 3.0, 2.0, 6.0
WIDTH, HEIGHT = 740, 500

COLORS = {
    'base': '#ff6b6b', 'link': '#7da6ff', 'pin': '#808080',
    'x': '#ef4444', 'y': '#22c55e', 'z': '#3b82f6'
}

# --- GEOMETRY UTILS ---

def apply_rotation(v, theta_deg=0, base_transform=None):
    """Applies base transform, rotation swing, and global Z-rotation."""
    if base_transform:
        v = np.array([base_transform(*p) for p in v])
    
    # Swing rotation (Link logic)
    rad = np.radians(theta_deg)
    v = np.array([
        [x, z*np.cos(rad) - y*np.sin(rad), z*np.sin(rad) + y*np.cos(rad)]
        for x, y, z in v
    ])
    
    # Global 90-deg CW rotation about Z
    return np.array([ (p[1], -p[0], p[2]) for p in v])

def get_cuboid_mesh(w, h, l, color, theta_deg=0, base_transform=None):
    # Local vertices
    v = np.array([
        [-w/2, -h/2, 0], [w/2, -h/2, 0], [w/2, h/2, 0], [-w/2, h/2, 0],
        [-w/2, -h/2, l], [w/2, -h/2, l], [w/2, h/2, l], [-w/2, h/2, l]
    ])
    v = apply_rotation(v, theta_deg, base_transform)
    
    faces = [[0,1,2,3],[4,5,6,7],[0,1,5,4],[2,3,7,6],[0,3,7,4],[1,2,6,5]]
    return [
        go.Mesh3d(
            x=v[f,0], y=v[f,1], z=v[f,2], i=[0,0], j=[1,2], k=[2,3],
            color=color, flatshading=True, opacity=1,
            lighting=dict(ambient=0.45, diffuse=0.8, roughness=0.1, specular=1),
            scene='scene'
        ) for f in faces
    ]

def get_joint_pin(w, h):
    r, nb = h/2, 50
    theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, nb)
    y_coords = np.linspace(-w/2 - 0.2, w/2 + 0.2, 2)
    T, Y = np.meshgrid(theta, y_coords)
    
    return go.Mesh3d(
        x=-(r*np.cos(T)).flatten(), y=Y.flatten(), z=(r*np.sin(T)).flatten(),
        color=COLORS['pin'], alphahull=0, flatshading=True, scene='scene'
    )

# --- VIZ BUILDER ---

def build_viz():
    fig = go.Figure()
    angles = np.arange(0, 181, 2)

    # Static Components
    base = get_cuboid_mesh(CUBOID_W, CUBOID_H, 5, COLORS['base'], base_transform=lambda x,y,z:(x,-z,y))
    pin = [get_joint_pin(CUBOID_W, CUBOID_H)]
    axis_line = [go.Scatter3d(x=[0,0], y=[-4.5,4.5], z=[0,0], mode='lines', 
                             line=dict(color='white', width=1.2), scene='scene', showlegend=False)]
    
    link_init = get_cuboid_mesh(CUBOID_W, CUBOID_H, LINK_L, COLORS['link'], theta_deg=angles[0])
    fig.add_traces(base + pin + axis_line + link_init)

    # Corner Axes (Scene 2)
    axes_conf = [
        (COLORS['x'], 'X', [1.2,0,0], [1.9,0,0]),
        (COLORS['y'], 'Y', [0,1.2,0], [0,1.9,0]),
        (COLORS['z'], 'Z', [0,0,1.2], [0,0,1.9])
    ]
    
    for color, label, v_line, v_text in axes_conf:
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter3d(
            x=[0, v_line[0], None, v_text[0]], 
            y=[0, v_line[1], None, v_text[1]], 
            z=[0, v_line[2], None, v_text[2]],
            mode='lines+text', text=["","","",label], textposition="middle center",
            line=dict(color=color, width=3), textfont=dict(color=color, size=14),
            scene='scene2', showlegend=False
        ))

    # Frames
    fig.frames = [
        go.Frame(
            data=base + pin + axis_line + get_cuboid_mesh(CUBOID_W, CUBOID_H, LINK_L, COLORS['link'], theta_deg=a),
            name=str(a), traces=list(range(len(base) + 2 + len(link_init)))
        ) for a in angles
    ]

    fig.update_layout(
        dragmode=False,
        scene=dict(
            domain=dict(x=[0,1], y=[0,1]),
            xaxis=dict(visible=False), yaxis=dict(visible=False), zaxis=dict(visible=False),
            aspectmode='data', camera=dict(eye=dict(x=-1.5, y=1.5, z=1.5)), bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)'
        ),
        scene2=dict(
            domain=dict(x=[0.8,0.98], y=[0.75,0.95]),
            xaxis=dict(visible=False, range=[-2,2]), 
            yaxis=dict(visible=False, range=[-2,2]), 
            zaxis=dict(visible=False, range=[-2,2]),
            camera=dict(eye=dict(x=1.5, y=1.5, z=1.5)), bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)'
        ),
        sliders=[dict(
            active=0, pad={"t":0,"b":50},
            currentvalue={"prefix":"θ: ","suffix":"°","font":{"color":"white","size":16}},
            tickcolor="white", font=dict(color="white"),
            steps=[dict(method="animate", label=str(a), 
                        args=[[str(a)], {"frame":{"duration":0,"redraw":True},"mode":"immediate"}]) for a in angles]
        )],
        paper_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)', margin=dict(l=0,r=0,t=0,b=0), width=WIDTH, height=HEIGHT
    )
    return fig

build_viz().show(config={'displayModeBar': False})


                            
                                            




Prismatic Joint (P)
A prismatic joint provides one degree of freedom, enabling linear motion along a single axis through sliding.


Code
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# --- CONFIGURATION ---
WIDTH, HEIGHT = 740, 500

# Big body
BODY_W, BODY_H, BODY_L = 2.5, 3.5 , 3.5

# Small slider
SLIDER_W, SLIDER_H, SLIDER_L = 1.2, 7.0 , 1.2

COLORS = {
    'body': '#ff6b6b',
    'slider': '#7da6ff',
    'rod': '#cccccc',
    'x': '#ef4444',
    'y': '#22c55e',
    'z': '#3b82f6'
}

# --- GEOMETRY ---

def cuboid_vertices(w, h, l):
    return np.array([
        [-w/2, -h/2, -l/2], [w/2, -h/2, -l/2],
        [w/2, h/2, -l/2],  [-w/2, h/2, -l/2],
        [-w/2, -h/2,  l/2], [w/2, -h/2,  l/2],
        [w/2, h/2,  l/2],  [-w/2, h/2,  l/2]
    ])

def apply_transform(v, d=0, base=False):
    if base:
        v = np.array([(z, x, y) for x, y, z in v])
    return np.array([[x, y + d, z] for x, y, z in v])

def get_cuboid(w, h, l, color, d=0, base=False):
    v = cuboid_vertices(w, h, l)
    v = apply_transform(v, d, base)
    faces = [[0,1,2,3],[4,5,6,7],[0,1,5,4],[2,3,7,6],[0,3,7,4],[1,2,6,5]]
    return [
        go.Mesh3d(
            x=v[f,0], y=v[f,1], z=v[f,2],
            i=[0,0], j=[1,2], k=[2,3],
            color=color, flatshading=True,
            lighting=dict(ambient=0.45, diffuse=0.8, roughness=0.2, specular=0.6),
            scene='scene'
        ) for f in faces
    ]

# --- BUILD ---

def build_viz():
    fig = go.Figure()
    displacements = np.linspace(-3, 3, 60)

    body = get_cuboid(BODY_W, BODY_H, BODY_L, COLORS['body'], base=True)
    slider_init = get_cuboid(SLIDER_W, SLIDER_H, SLIDER_L, COLORS['slider'], d=0)
    rod = go.Scatter3d(
        x=[0,0], y=[-6,6], z=[0,0],
        mode='lines', line=dict(color=COLORS['rod'], width=4),
        showlegend=False, scene='scene'
    )

    fig.add_traces(body + [rod] + slider_init)

    axes_conf = [
        (COLORS['x'], 'X', [1.2,0,0], [1.9,0,0]),
        (COLORS['y'], 'Y', [0,1.2,0], [0,1.9,0]),
        (COLORS['z'], 'Z', [0,0,1.2], [0,0,1.9])
    ]

    for color, label, v_line, v_text in axes_conf:
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter3d(
            x=[0, v_line[0], None, v_text[0]],
            y=[0, v_line[1], None, v_text[1]],
            z=[0, v_line[2], None, v_text[2]],
            mode='lines+text', text=["","","",label], textposition="middle center",
            line=dict(color=color, width=3), textfont=dict(color=color, size=14),
            scene='scene2', showlegend=False
        ))

    fig.frames = [
        go.Frame(
            data=body + [rod] + get_cuboid(SLIDER_W, SLIDER_H, SLIDER_L, COLORS['slider'], d=d),
            name=f"{d:.2f}",
            traces=list(range(len(body) + 1 + len(slider_init)))
        ) for d in displacements
    ]

    fig.update_layout(
        dragmode=False,
        scene=dict(
            domain=dict(x=[0, 1], y=[0, 1]), # Centered horizontally and vertically
            xaxis=dict(visible=False, range=[-7, 7]),
            yaxis=dict(visible=False, range=[-7, 7]),
            zaxis=dict(visible=False, range=[-7, 7]),
            aspectmode='data',
            camera=dict(
                eye=dict(x=-1.5, y=1.5, z=1.5), # Pulled in slightly
                center=dict(x=0, y=0, z=0)      # Explicitly look at center
            ),
            bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)'
        ),
        scene2=dict(
            domain=dict(x=[0.85, 0.98], y=[0.8, 0.95]), # Tucked in the top right
            xaxis=dict(visible=False, range=[-2, 2]),
            yaxis=dict(visible=False, range=[-2, 2]),
            zaxis=dict(visible=False, range=[-2, 2]),
            camera=dict(eye=dict(x=1.5, y=1.5, z=1.5)),
            bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)'
        ),
        sliders=[dict(
            active=len(displacements)//2,
            pad={"t":0,"b":40},
            currentvalue={"prefix":"d: ", "suffix":" units", "font":{"color":"white","size":16}},
            tickcolor="white", font=dict(color="white"),
            steps=[dict(method="animate", label=f"{d:.1f}", 
                        args=[[f"{d:.2f}"], {"frame":{"duration":0,"redraw":True},"mode":"immediate"}]) 
                   for d in displacements]
        )],
        paper_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)', margin=dict(l=0,r=0,t=0,b=0),
        width=WIDTH, height=HEIGHT
    )

    return fig

build_viz().show(config={'displayModeBar': False})


                            
                                            




Cylindrical Joint (C)
A cylindrical joint provides two degrees of freedom, combining rotation and translation along the same axis.


Universal Joint (U)
A universal joint provides two degrees of freedom, allowing rotation about two perpendicular axes.


Spherical Joint (S)
A spherical joint provides three degrees of freedom, enabling rotation about three perpendicular axes like a ball-and-socket.
